hook: A medieval refuse-and-sewage gap squeezed between two rows of old-town houses, opened up as an archaeological window with a key from the Stadthaus.
facts: An Ehgraben is the narrow open gap left between adjoining house rows in the medieval old town, used for refuse and waste-water disposal.
This window sits between the Kirchgasse and Rössligasse house rows, with entrances between Schifflände 30/32 or Oberdorfstrasse 25/27.
Information panels explain the history of waste and waste-water disposal in Zurich's old town.
Access requires a key borrowed at counter S of the Stadthaus, Stadthausquai 17 (Mon-Fri 08:00-18:00, Sat 09:00-12:30); a valid ID is left as deposit, max 3-day loan.
It is one of the City of Zurich's roughly ten 'archaeological windows' that present finds at their original site.
w: Borrow a key from the Stadthaus and let yourself into a sliver of medieval Zurich: the Ehgraben, the refuse-and-sewage gap that once ran between the old-town houses. Centuries of waste went down these slots before drains existed. An information window now lights the cramped void. The city's plumbing history, behind a locked door.

hook: A fragment of Zurich's 13th-century city wall preserved in a cellar under the Central Library, opened to visitors as a key-accessed archaeological window.
facts: The cellar at Chorgasse 22 preserves remains of the city wall from the 13th century together with the city's first water pipeline.
It is one of the City of Zurich's archaeological windows, presenting finds at their original location.
Access requires a key borrowed at counter S of the Stadthaus, Stadthausquai 17 (Mon-Fri 08:00-18:00, Sat 09:00-12:30); a valid ID is left as deposit, max 3-day loan, no advance reservation.
The cellar lies beneath the Zentralbibliothek (Central Library) building in the Niederdorf old town east of the Limmat.
w: In a cellar under the Central Library, reached with a borrowed key, a chunk of Zurich's 13th-century city wall still stands, alongside a stretch of the town's first piped water supply. Few cities let you unlock their medieval defences yourself. Down the steps, the rampart waits in the dark. A fortress wall, filed under quiet basement.

hook: A medieval room at 'Zum Brunnenhof' with some of Europe's oldest surviving secular wall paintings, made around 1330 for a Jewish household and inscribed in Hebrew.
facts: The late-Gothic murals at Brunngasse 8 ('Zum Brunnenhof') date to around 1330 and were uncovered during a 1996 renovation.
They are documented as the only surviving secular Jewish room interior of that period in Europe.
Heraldic coats of arms in the paintings carry Hebrew inscriptions; around 1330 the house was home to a Jewish family (Minne and her sons Moses and Mordechai ben Menachem).
Scenes include a courtly round dance drawing on the Neidhart minnesinger tradition, stylistically related to the Manesse Codex illuminated in Zurich in those same years.
It is one of Zurich's archaeological windows; a key is borrowed at counter S of the Stadthaus, Stadthausquai 17 (ID as deposit, max 3-day loan).
w: Behind a locked door on Brunngasse hide some of Europe's oldest secular wall paintings, dancing knights and coats of arms brushed onto the plaster around 1330. One bears Hebrew script; the room is thought to be the only surviving medieval Jewish domestic interior of its kind on the continent. Borrow the key and step into the 14th century.

hook: A free, round-the-clock archaeological window inside the Opéra underground car park displaying finds from a 5,000-year-old lake-dweller pile-dwelling settlement, part of a UNESCO World Heritage site.
facts: Building the Opéra car park beneath Sechseläutenplatz was halted for about nine months while around 50 archaeologists excavated the prehistoric lakeshore settlement.
More than 20,000 archaeological objects were recovered, with cultural layers dating back over 5,000 years.
Among the finds is one of the world's oldest preserved wooden doors.
The prehistoric pile dwellings around the Zurich lake basin are inscribed as part of the UNESCO World Heritage 'Prehistoric Pile Dwellings around the Alps'.
The archaeological window inside the car park displays original finds and sediment layers and is freely accessible around the clock.
w: Two floors down in an ordinary opera-house car park, a glass window opens onto five-thousand-year-old Zurich: the stilt-village finds dug up when the garage was built. Among the 20,000 objects sits one of the world's oldest wooden doors. UNESCO-listed, free, and open around the clock. Prehistory, parked beneath the cars.

hook: 200-year-old wine tavern where you earn a carved name by climbing two ceiling beams and drinking wine upside down — the Balkenprobe.
facts: Located at Rindermarkt 12 in Zürich's Niederdorf, first documented as a wine tavern in 1801.
The building's name ('apple chamber') comes from the neighbouring St. Verena convent's nuns, who once dried apples there.

Its signature ritual, the Balkenprobe, requires a guest to climb over two ceiling beams and drink a glass of white wine while hanging upside down; those who succeed may carve their name into the wood panelling, tables and chairs.
Author Gottfried Keller (1819-1890) grew up across the street at the Rindermarkt and is closely associated with the tavern.
The restaurant closed abruptly in autumn 2024 when its operators cited debts, then reopened in early 2025 under new operator Andreas Himstedt with chef Karim Schumann, retaining the Balkenprobe.
legend: Local lore holds that Keller was a regular who nearly missed his appointment as Zürich city clerk because of the tavern's wine, though Keller never mentions the Oepfelchammer in his writings or letters.
w: At this two-hundred-year-old tavern, drinkers still attempt the Balkenprobe: clamber up the ceiling beams, hang upside down and empty a glass of wine without spilling, then sign the wall. The poet Gottfried Keller is said to have been a regular. Briefly shut in 2024, it reopened in 2025, beams intact. Earn your wine the hard way.

hook: A hushed museum of hyper-realistic wax moulages — three-dimensional models of diseased skin and bodies once used to teach medicine.
facts: Run jointly by the University of Zürich (UZH) and the University Hospital Zürich (USZ), at Haldenbachstrasse 14, 8091 Zürich.
Moulages are naturalistic wax casts of pathological changes to the body, made and used in clinics since the 19th century to study and document disease.
The Zürich collection holds roughly 2,500 moulages, of which about 600 are on display; it is regarded as among the best-preserved and most lifelike such collections in the world.
Opening hours are limited — Wednesday afternoons and Saturday afternoons, with other times by arrangement — and admission is free.
w: A quiet university room lined with wax: hundreds of moulages modelling diseased skin, pox and tumours in unnerving, lifelike detail, cast when photography could not yet teach medicine. The craft was a Zurich speciality. Open a couple of afternoons a week and free, it draws the morbidly curious. Dermatology as waxwork, beautiful and grim.

hook: Possibly the world's most beautiful police-station lobby — a vaulted entrance hall drowned in Augusto Giacometti's red-and-ochre flower frescoes.
facts: The frescoed entrance hall is inside Amtshaus I, the Zürich city police building at Bahnhofquai 3, 8001 Zürich.
The City of Zürich held a competition in 1922 to brighten the gloomy hall; painter Augusto Giacometti (1877-1947) won and executed the work in al fresco and al secco technique between 1923 and 1925.
The walls depict figures at work — stone carvers and carpenters for the crafts, astronomers and magicians for science — while ceiling and vault carry ornamental floral and geometric patterns.
Locals nicknamed it the 'Blüemlihalle' (little-flower hall) for its countless painted flowers; it was extensively restored in 2019 and is considered of national importance.
The hall is freely accessible to the public during the police building's opening hours.
w: The most beautiful entrance hall in Zurich belongs to a police station. In the 1920s Augusto Giacometti drowned the lobby of Amtshaus I in blazing orange and blue blossom — the Blueemlihalle, the hall of little flowers. Walk straight in off Bahnhofquai; it is public and free. The cousin of the more famous Alberto, painting cops a garden.

hook: A 51-metre concrete observatory tower over the old town, still pointing a 12-tonne 1907 Zeiss telescope at the night sky for paying visitors.
facts: Switzerland's oldest public observatory, opened for operation on 15 June 1907 near Werdmühleplatz / Uraniastrasse in Zürich's Altstadt.
At 51 metres it was the first building in Zürich built from concrete and has been a protected monument since 1989.
Its refractor uses a Fraunhofer two-lens system of 30 cm aperture and 5.05 m focal length, built by Carl Zeiss in Jena, weighing about 12 tonnes.
The telescope was dismantled in March 2006 for a full restoration in Jena and re-hoisted into the dome in April 2007, reopening in May 2007.
The observatory still runs paid public guided viewings in clear weather, typically Tuesday to Friday evenings from 20:00.
w: A slim 51-metre tower of 1907 concrete rises over the old town, built so city-dwellers could put an eye to a real telescope. The Urania observatory still opens on clear evenings, its century-old lens trained on Saturn's rings above the roofs. Climb up after dark and let an astronomer find you a planet. Stargazing, the old-fashioned way.

hook: A 15-metre stone slingshot rising over a city park — half public sculpture, half children's swing that glows orange when you sit on it.
facts: The monumental sculpture 'Y' by Kosovo-born artist Sislej Xhafa (b. 1970) stands in Hardaupark in Zürich-West and was unveiled in 2011.
It was the final work realised under the 'Kunst Öffentlichkeit Zürich' research project of the Zürich University of the Arts and the City of Zürich.
Built as a steel structure clad in a plastic shell, it doubles as a swing for neighbourhood children and illuminates in orange and yellow when occupied.

Xhafa frames it as a symbol of resistance — the biblical slingshot of David against Goliath and a weapon of the oppressed in modern street protest — with the English pronunciation of 'Y' ('why') inviting the viewer to question.
A move to remove the sculpture was rejected; it was confirmed to remain in place.
w: A fifteen-metre catapult of steel looms over a Zurich-West playground, a giant slingshot — or perhaps a swing — by the Kosovan artist Sislej Xhafa. Locals read it as a question and as a monument to resistance, the small firing back at the big. Children clamber on it; nobody quite agrees what it means. Public art with a loaded grin.

hook: The world's oldest continuously operating vegetarian restaurant — meat-free since 1898, now serving over 100 dishes from a buzzing buffet.
facts: Founded on 3 July 1898 in Zürich as the 'Vegetarierheim', it is recognised by Guinness World Records as the oldest vegetarian restaurant in the world.
It moved to its long-standing address at Sihlstrasse 28 on 22 November 1899.
German tailor Ambrosius Hiltl took to vegetarianism after rheumatism led him to the diet of Swiss doctor Maximilian Bircher-Benner; his family has run the restaurant for four generations.
Today it offers more than 100 vegetarian and vegan dishes, with all wine served being vegan.
w: Recognised by Guinness as the world's oldest vegetarian restaurant, Hiltl has fed Zurich meat-free since 1898, when meat-free struck many as faintly mad. Once mocked as the 'root mine,' it now runs a vast pay-by-weight buffet from morning to night. Four generations of one family kept the doors open. Vegetarianism, long before it was cool.

hook: Beneath the Zürichberg woods, a bronze James Joyce sits cross-legged with book and cigarette, eternally beside the zoo whose lions he liked to hear.
facts: Irish writer James Joyce (1882-1941), author of Ulysses, is buried in Fluntern Cemetery in Zürich's Zürichberg district, where he died in January 1941.
His grave is marked by a seated bronze statue by sculptor Milton Hebald (installed 1966), showing Joyce cross-legged with an open book and a cigarette, his cane at his side.
His wife Nora Barnacle and son Giorgio are also buried in the same cemetery.
The cemetery sits beside Zürich Zoo and is reached via the terminus of Tram 6.
legend: Visitors and admirers note that the grave overlooks the nearby zoo, and it is often said Joyce would have appreciated being able to 'hear the lions roar'.
w: The author of Ulysses lies in Fluntern cemetery, on the hill where he died in 1941, watched over by a life-size bronze of himself, seated, legs crossed, book in hand. The city zoo sits just uphill; admirers like to think Joyce, a lover of cats and noise, can still hear the lions roar. Bring a line of his to read aloud.

hook: A modular urban garden and bar built from stacked, brightly painted shipping containers in the shadow of the Prime Tower.
facts: Opened in summer 2012 on the Gerold-Areal in Zurich's District 5, on land leased temporarily from the city.
Built largely from used shipping containers, partly because the lease was originally short-term and containers were a quick, removable construction method.
Sits beside the Hardbrücke viaduct at the foot of the Prime Tower, directly behind the Freitag flagship tower.
Includes its own vegetable garden, a fresh kitchen, small shops and pop-up exhibition spaces, and operates as both a summer garden and a heated winter version.
Address is Geroldstrasse 23/23a, 8005 Zürich.
w: Stacked shipping containers, vegetable beds, fairy lights and deckchairs squeezed under the railway viaduct in Zurich West. Since 2012 Frau Gerold's Garden has been the neighbourhood's open-air living room — drinks, street food and seasonal art. In winter it turns into a fondue chalet. Gritty industrial Zurich at its most playful.

hook: A 26-metre tower of stacked rusty freight containers housing the Freitag flagship store, with a free public viewing terrace over Zurich West.
facts: Built in 2006 from 19 stacked rusty freight containers, rising about 26 metres (85 ft) beside the Hardbrücke in Zürich West.
The containers were sourced from Hamburg and transported to Zurich by train.
Houses the Freitag flagship store over four floors, selling the brand's bags made from recycled truck tarpaulins.
A viewing platform several floors above the shop is open during store hours and looks out over Zürich West, the Prime Tower and toward Uetliberg.
Often cited as one of the tallest shipping-container structures of its kind; address is Geroldstrasse 17, 8005 Zürich.
w: The Freitag brothers stacked nineteen rusted shipping containers into a twenty-six-metre tower in 2006, part flagship shop, part lookout. Climb the steel stairwell for a free view across the tracks and rooftops of creative Zurich West. The shop below sells bags cut from used truck tarpaulins. Recycling, raised to a landmark.

hook: A fantastical, dreamlike sculpture park of giant winged dogs, serpents and surreal creatures built over decades by Swiss artist Bruno Weber.
facts: Created by Swiss artist Bruno Weber (1931-2011) as a lifelong Gesamtkunstwerk, with work beginning in 1962 on his family estate on the Weinberg hill.
Covers about 20,000 m2 and straddles the municipalities of Dietikon and Spreitenbach, west of Zurich.
After decades of disputes with authorities over unauthorised structures, all its buildings received official approval in 1998 as a special zone for artistic creation.
A water garden (Wassergarten) opened in May 2012, flanked by two roughly 100-metre 'winged dog' (Flügelhund) figures forming a footbridge.
The park closed for financial reasons on 20 October 2014, then reopened on 4 April 2015 after a public campaign and a new board of trustees; it remains open in 2026 (typically Wed/Sat/Sun).
Coordinate sits just west of the catalogue's core Zurich bbox minimum but within the build's 0.1-degree margin; address Zur Weinrebe 3, 8953 Dietikon.
w: On a hillside in Dietikon, the artist Bruno Weber spent half a century raising a private dream-world: dragons, winged dog-beasts and a columned water garden, all hand-built in painted concrete. He lived among his creatures until 2011; anyone can visit now. Equal parts Gaudi and fever dream. Switzerland's strangest back garden.

hook: Le Corbusier's last building: a steel-and-glass pavilion clad in multicoloured enamelled panels by the lake at Zürichhorn.
facts: Le Corbusier's final building, commissioned in 1960 by Swiss art collector and patron Heidi Weber and completed in 1967.
Constructed from prefabricated steel elements combined with multi-coloured enamelled steel plates, with a distinctive floating roof.
Inaugurated on 15 July 1967, two years after Le Corbusier's death in 1965.
Since 2019 it has been managed by the Museum für Gestaltung Zürich on behalf of the City of Zurich.
Open seasonally (roughly May to November); located at Höschgasse 8, 8008 Zürich, next to the Chinese Garden.
w: Le Corbusier's very last building turned its back on his usual raw concrete: a crisp lakeside box of steel, glass and enamel in primary colours, finished in 1967, two years after his death. He designed it whole — architecture, furniture and art under one bright roof. Now a little museum to the man himself. Modernism, in full colour.

hook: A free greenhouse collection of thousands of cacti and succulents near the lakeshore at Mythenquai, one of the world's largest of its kind.
facts: Formally established in 1931, it is one of the largest and most important succulent collections in the world.
Holds more than 4,400 species across roughly 78 plant families, totalling over 20,000 cacti and succulent plants.
Displayed in seven greenhouses plus an outdoor rockery garden.
Admission is free, and the greenhouses and grounds are open daily including Sundays and holidays.
Located at Mythenquai 88, 8002 Zürich, near the Brunaustrasse/Sukkulentensammlung tram and bus stops.
w: Behind glass by the lakeshore grows one of the world's great hoards of succulents and cacti, some 4,500 species of swollen, spined plants, free to wander among. The collection took root in 1931 from a single private donation. Step inside the warm houses and the desert blooms. A prickly paradise hiding in plain sight on Mythenquai.

hook: An ornate temple-style Chinese garden by the lake, gifted to Zurich by its partner city Kunming as thanks for help with Kunming's water supply.
facts: A gift from Zurich's Chinese partner city Kunming, thanking Zurich for technical and scientific help developing Kunming's drinking-water supply and drainage.
Built from May to October 1993 by horticultural experts and craftsmen from both Kunming and Zurich, and financed by Zurich's water utility on its 125th anniversary.
Inaugurated in spring 1994 by the mayors of Kunming and Zurich (then Josef Estermann).
Designed around the theme of the 'Three Friends of Winter' (pine, bamboo and plum), with a central pond and island and over 500 painted still lifes and landscapes in its galleries.
Considered one of the highest-ranking Chinese gardens outside China; open seasonally (roughly mid-March to mid-October) near the Zürichhorn, beside the Pavillon Le Corbusier.
w: Zurich's sister city Kunming sent it a garden, and in 1994 this jewel of red pavilions, carved bridges and still water opened by the lake. Built to the old Chinese ideal of the 'three friends of winter' — pine, bamboo and plum — it repays a slow, quiet circuit. A precise piece of China, gifted in thanks. Harmony, drawn to a plan.

hook: Zurich's oldest riverside bath, a 100-metre swimming channel in the Limmat where bathers drift downstream with the current.
facts: Built in 1909 by architects Fissler and Friedrich, it is the oldest riverside bath in the city of Zurich.
Features a roughly 100-metre swimming channel in the Limmat where swimmers float downstream with the current, plus a non-swimmer pool, children's paddling pool and a diving platform.
Extended in 1956 with a non-swimmer pool and paddling pool to plans by Elsa and Ernst Burkhardt.
Originally reserved exclusively for men, with a women's area and pool added later at the upper end of the facility.
Entry is free; it operates seasonally in summer and hosts evening cultural events and open-air cinema.
w: On hot days half the city drifts down the Limmat at Unterer Letten, a free riverside bath open since 1909. Swimmers ride the green current along a long channel, haul out, then walk back to do it again, between lawns, kiosks and diving boards. The water runs clean and cold off the lake. Wild city swimming, civilised the Swiss way.

hook: Zurich's largest cemetery is also its largest park, a 29-hectare green oasis dotted with the graves of Henry Dunant, Johanna Spyri and Gottfried Keller.
facts: Opened in 1877, Sihlfeld is the largest cemetery in the city of Zurich at roughly 288,000 m2, making it also the largest contiguous green space and a popular park for walkers.
Switzerland's first crematorium was built here in 1889, a pioneering facility for the time.
Notable graves include Red Cross founder Henry Dunant, 'Heidi' author Johanna Spyri, writers Gottfried Keller and Max Frisch, and composer Arthur Honegger.
The grounds contain family mausoleums and gravestones protected as historical monuments, ranging in style from Art Nouveau to modernist, with marble and basalt obelisks and angelic sculptures.
w: Zurich's largest cemetery doubles as one of its loveliest parks: forty hectares of old trees, paths and grand 19th-century tombs in Wiedikon. Johanna Spyri, who wrote Heidi, lies among the residents, alongside city fathers and forgotten guildsmen. Joggers and mourners share the avenues. A garden of the dead that the living quietly love.

hook: A wild little island in the Limmat where Zurich comes to swim, sunbathe and forage wild garlic in spring, ringed by a free public river pool.
facts: Werdinsel is a river island in the Limmat in the district of Höngg, northwest Zurich, roughly 550 m long and 125 m wide, covering about 0.06 km2.
The island hosts the Flussbad Au-Höngg, one of four free public river baths in the Limmat within the city of Zurich; entry is free but bathing infrastructure is limited.
The Limmat flows past one side of the island while a calmer canal good for swimming runs along the other, with a tree-shaded strip of lawn in between.
On peak summer days the bathing area draws up to about 4,500 visitors.
w: A wooded island splits the Limmat at the city's western edge, reachable on foot, surprisingly wild. In spring its floor turns green with wild garlic; in summer the gravel banks fill with swimmers and naturists at the tip. Kingfishers work the channels. A scrap of real countryside that never left the city. Bring a towel or a basket.

n: Neuer Botanischer Garten der Universität Zürich
hook: Three glass domes on the Zürichberg slope shelter tropical, subtropical and savannah climates inside the University of Zurich's botanical garden.
facts: The garden opened in 1977 at Zollikerstrasse 107 in the Weinegg quarter, on roughly 53,000 m2, cultivating around 7,000 plant species.
Its signature feature is a trio of hemispherical greenhouse domes with diameters of about 27 m, 23 m and 20 m, each set to a different climate: tropical, subtropical and savannah.
It is run by the University of Zurich and is free to enter.
It replaced the cramped Alter Botanischer Garten zur Katz in the old town, which the university had outgrown.
w: Three glassy domes rise from a hillside above Seefeld, sheltering rainforest, savanna and cloud-forest under one futuristic skin. The university laid out these grounds in the 1970s, free to all, with thousands of species across the slope. Wander from the tropics to the alpine rockery in ten minutes. Botany under bubbles, and no ticket.

hook: A perfectly preserved baroque terraced garden hidden behind a patrician palais on the Hirschengraben, open to the public above the old town.
facts: The garden was laid out alongside the Palais Rechberg (Haus zum Rechberg), built 1759-1770, and appears on city maps from 1788-1793.
It covers about 4,000 m2 and is considered one of Zurich's finest baroque gardens, later partly anglicised with grottoes and curved paths between 1839 and 1866.
The Canton of Zurich acquired the property around 1899 and passed it to the University of Zurich, which owns and maintains it; the garden has been publicly accessible since then.
It is a protected monument and includes an orangery, a greenhouse and a fountain with sculptures by Johann Baptist Babel.
w: Slip through a gate off Hirschengraben and a formal baroque garden unfolds behind the patrician Haus zum Rechberg — clipped hedges, a fountain, espaliered fruit and benches few tourists ever find. Laid out in the 18th century, it offers a pocket of green calm a minute from the bustle. Pure old-town secret. Pack a book and a sandwich.

hook: The romantic landscaped park around the exotic Villa Patumbah, a Sumatra-tobacco fortune's mansion, now a free public garden in Seefeld.
facts: The park surrounds Villa Patumbah at Zollikerstrasse 128 in the Seefeld/Riesbach district and is publicly accessible during daytime hours year-round.
Its grounds were designed in 1890/1891 by landscape architect Evariste Mertens.
The villa was built 1883-1885 for a family that made its fortune in the Sumatra tobacco trade; 'Patumbah' refers to a place in Sumatra.
The restored villa today houses the Heimatschutzzentrum (heritage centre) of the Swiss Heritage Society.
w: The garden of a tobacco baron, opened to the public: Villa Patumbah was raised in the 1880s by a planter who had made his fortune in Sumatra, and named for his estate there. Its park keeps exotic trees, shaded corners and a faded grandeur. The villa now holds a heritage centre. A private Eden in Seefeld, handed to everyone.

hook: Below Höngg's hilltop church, terraced city vineyards spill toward the Limmat, the anchor of a wine trail recalling 600 years of Zurich viticulture.
facts: The Rebberg Chillesteig is the most prominent vineyard in Höngg, on the slope just below the Reformed Church, with which it forms a landmark visual unit seen from the Limmat valley.

The city of Zurich newly established the Chillesteig vineyard below the church in 1969; its roughly 3 hectares are managed by the municipal Juchhof estate of Grün Stadt Zürich and yield about 20,000 bottles of Zurich city wine a year.
Wine was Höngg's most important economic sector for some 600 years; the slope between village and river was once entirely planted with vines.

The Weinweg Höngg, a themed wine trail through grape- and wine-related sites in the district, was created from 2010 by Max Furrer and helpers and opened in 2012; its information board stands by the Höngg local museum.
w: Zurich still grows its own wine: the Chillesteig vineyard tips down the slope below Hongg's reformed church, vines looking out over the city and lake. A marked wine path threads the terraces, and the parish has pressed grapes here for centuries. Come at harvest and the whole hillside smells of must. Proof the city was once farmland too.

hook: An artificial hill of campus excavation spoil, now the high point of Irchel Park with benches, a copse and a panorama over Zurich.
facts: Monte Diggelmann is the highest point of Irchel Park, on the western slope of the Zürichberg in the Oberstrass district.
It was created in the 1970s from excavation material dug out for the University of Zurich's Irchel campus.
The hill is named after Walter Diggelmann, a former president of the Oberstrass neighbourhood association.
Its crown of trees and benches gives views over the city and the Limmat valley toward the airport and the Lägern ridge; in winter children use the slope for sledging.
w: A tongue-in-cheek name for a modest grassy knoll by the university's Irchel park, christened after a local figure as if it were an Alp. The gentle climb earns a calm, unfussy panorama over northern Zurich, best with a picnic. Students and dog-walkers have it mostly to themselves. A mountain in name, a breather in fact.

hook: A balcony on the Käferberg above Höngg with a sweeping view over the whole city and lake, said by some to beat the Uetliberg panorama.
facts: Die Waid is a viewpoint on Zurich's Käferberg, above the Höngg quarter, opening a broad panorama over the city with Lake Zurich behind it.
On clear days the snow-capped Alps of central Switzerland are visible from the lookout.
The adjacent restaurant Die Waid lets visitors take in the view over coffee and cake or dinner.
It can be reached on a one-to-two-hour walk from the city centre through Wipkingen and Höngg, or shortened by taking bus line 69 to the Waidbadstrasse stop.
w: From a terrace on the Kaferberg slope above Hongg, the whole of Zurich lays itself out — the lake, the old town, and on a clear day the Alps along the horizon. Allotment gardens tumble below and a restaurant waits behind. A short hop from the centre by tram and funicular, and one of the best free views in town. Sunset seats, no charge.
